Communicate effectively through writing, speaking, listening, reading, and interpersonal abilities.

Organize and create a checklist of production steps, procedures, requirements to establish a set with departments listed below, operate working set departments, and close a production location.
Creative Department: Secure Intellectual Property, including script, book, film concept and Secure screenwriter, cast, and director.
Business Development: Secure attorney and set up business entity (e.g., S-Corp, LLC, LP), Set up federal and state taxes and reporting, and Secure film financing.

Pre-Production: Hire cast and crew; Secure locations, set designs, production equipment, and all physical elements necessary to shoot the project; and Initiate tracking and recording budget expenses, and payroll.

Principal Photography (Production): Film the project with cast and shooting crew; Create and film marketing materials (e.g., Electronic Press Kit {EPK}); Secure ongoing releases (e.g., clearances, deal memos, union paperwork); and Track and record ongoing payroll and expenses, including petty cash.

Production wrap: Return all film items used during production; Restore film locations to original conditions; File insurance claims for lost and damaged items; and Finalize all receipts, complete film accounting.

Post-Production; Complete editing, sound design, coloring, music; Complete paper deliverables (all documentation, clearances, union paperwork); and Finalize payroll and expenses, including petty cash.

Distribution: Pitch completed film to distributors, markets, and film festivals; Negotiate distribution deal(s); Deliver tech and paper deliverables per distributor; and Deliver marketing materials (e.g., stills, EPK, one-sheet, marketing blurbs,).

Diagram and explain the film production departments, such as construction, wardrobe, electrical, art, sound, camera, assistant directors, and office to document interdepartmental relationships for working seamlessly.

Demonstrate understanding of how creative and technical elements translate from principal photography to post-production.

Analyze and simulate an application of terminology and processes within film production departments.

Development Phase: a. Describe and apply the terminology and processes for script writing, b. Describe and apply the terminology and processes for casting, and c. Describe and apply the terminology and processes for business and financing structures.

Pre-Production Phase: a. Describe and apply the terminology and processes for location scouts; b. Describe and apply the terminology and processes for art, including construction, props, and set dressing; c. Describe and apply the terminology and processes for costumes; and d. Describe and apply the terminology and processes for hair and makeup.

Principal Photography Phase: a. Describe and apply the terminology and processes for grip, electrical, and sound; b. Describe and apply the terminology and processes for special effects, sounds effects, music supervision, mixing, or CGI in post-production; c. Describe and apply the terminology and processes for camera and acting in front of camera; and d. Describe and apply the terminology and processes for on-set production.

Post-Production Phase: a. Describe and apply the terminology and processes for picture edit, b. Describe and apply the terminology and processes for sound edit, and c. Describe and apply the terminology and processes for final product export.

Distribution/ Marketing Phase: a. Describe and apply the terminology and processes for distributors, film markets, and festivals; b. Describe and apply the terminology and processes for marketing materials; and c. Describe and apply the terminology and processes for return on investment (ROI).
